# Writing Anti-Patterns

## Summary

This page captures recurring writing failures that make agent-generated website and app copy sound generic, self-referential, or internally focused.

## Meta-Narration

The agent describes the section instead of writing the section.

Examples of failure modes:

- text that explains what the section is about instead of making the point
- copy that sounds like design annotations or product notes
- headings that label a content bucket without user meaning

## Internal-Language Drift

- roadmap language shown to users
- implementation terms shown to customers without a reason
- company-centric phrasing where user-centric phrasing is needed

## Empty Professionalism

- inflated adjectives without evidence
- vague promises with no mechanism
- polished but low-information copy
- corporate phrasing that sounds safe but says little

## Weak Action Language

- generic CTAs
- passive voice around actions
- sections that never resolve into a user decision or next step

## Scan Failure

- long, padded openings
- buried key claims
- headings that do not help users decide whether to keep reading
- paragraphs doing more than one job

## Correction Rule

When a draft sounds like it is explaining the page from the outside, rewrite it from the user's point of view:

- what can they do
- why should they care
- what changes for them
- what should they do next

## Best Skill Pairings

- `copywriting`: use when the copy needs to be rebuilt around user value rather than internal description
- `copy-editing`: use when the draft exists but still sounds meta, padded, or internally focused
- `ux-writing`: use when the same anti-patterns appear in interface copy rather than marketing copy
